data.title: Longest bond market drawdown on record hits 68 months
data.description:
The U.S. bond market is mired in its longest losing streak ever recorded, with no end in sight.

Charlie Bilello said the bond market has now been in drawdown for 68 months as of March 2026, “by far the longest in history.” The milestone puts the post-2020 bond bear market in a category all its own.

In a sign of a structural repricing, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index drawdown started in August 2020 and has persisted much longer than previous fixed-income slumps, which historically burned out within a year or so.

Bonds absorbed the initial blow from the pandemic-induced inflation shock, then weathered the Federal Reserve’s fastest tightening cycle in decades. Now, they face a market that keeps questioning how low rates can really go, as the war in Iran sends energy costs surging.

The question for bond-market investors is whether sticky inflation and a new oil shock keep the post-2020 drawdown alive even longer, as Fed officials assess energy-driven price pressures bleeding into broader inflation and near-term expectations.

data.title: AeroVironment selected for U.S. Navy ISR services contract
data.description:
AeroVironment (AVAV) on Tuesday said it was selected by the U.S. Navy to participate in a contractor-owned, contractor-operated intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance services program aimed at expanding and modernizing naval capabilities.

Under the initiative, AeroVironment (AVAV) will compete with other industry partners for delivery orders to provide ISR services supporting naval operations. The program is structured to deliver persistent surveillance using a mix of autonomous systems, integrated sensors and operational support.

Program structure and scope

The contract vehicle allows the Navy to procure ISR services rather than directly owning and operating all equipment. Companies selected for the program are expected to provide turnkey capabilities, including aircraft, sensors and mission support personnel.

AeroVironment (AVAV) said its offering will include its JUMP 20-X uncrewed aircraft system, a Group 3 vertical take-off and landing platform designed for both land and maritime missions.

Aircraft capabilities

The JUMP 20-X system is designed to operate without traditional launch and recovery equipment, which can reduce logistical requirements in constrained environments. The aircraft has an endurance of more than 13 hours, a range of about 115 miles and can carry payloads of up to 30 pounds (13.61 kg).

The platform supports multiple sensor configurations, allowing it to be adapted for different ISR missions, including maritime surveillance and ground-based reconnaissance.

Prior deployments

AeroVironment (AVAV) has previously provided ISR-related support to several U.S. and allied units, including operations tied to U.S. Naval Forces Southern Command, the 22nd Marine Expeditionary Unit and the Korean Navy.

The company did not disclose the potential value of the Navy program or the number of competitors selected.

The award reflects the Pentagon’s broader push to expand unmanned and contractor-operated ISR capabilities as part of efforts to increase operational flexibility and reduce the burden on uniformed personnel.

data.title: Phelan Green selects Honeywell tech for green jet fuel facility in South Africa
data.description:
A subsidiary of Phelan Green on Wednesday said it selected fuel-processing technology from Honeywell International (HON) for a planned sustainable aviation fuel facility in South Africa, as developers move forward with one of the country’s largest green energy projects.

Phelan eFuels said it will use Honeywell UOP’s Fischer-Tropsch Unicracking process at a proposed site in Saldanha Bay in the Western Cape. The technology is designed to convert synthetic feedstocks derived from carbon dioxide into electro-sustainable aviation fuel, or eSAF, that meets industry specifications.

The facility is part of the broader Phelan Green Hydrogen Project, a private investment valued at about 47 billion rand, or roughly $2.5 billion. South African authorities have designated the initiative as a strategic industrial development aimed at expanding the country’s role in low-carbon energy production.

Once operational, the project is expected to produce more than 140,000 metric tons of eSAF annually, with output primarily targeted for export to European Union and U.K. markets, where demand for lower-emissions aviation fuel is rising.

Construction of the facility is scheduled to begin in the fourth quarter of 2026.

data.title: Consumer staples win on defensive bets in Q1; Bunge, ADM lead gains
data.description:
U.S. equities closed Q1 on a weak footing, with the benchmark S&P 500 posting a quarterly decline of ~4.6%, while consumer staples emerged as a rare pocket of strength as investors rotated into defensive sectors.

The consumer sector only lagged the top-performing energy sector (XLE) and materials (XLB) in Q1 2026.

The XLP saw an outflow of approximately $6M, which compares to a massive $30B outflow from the broader S&P 500 (SPY) fund. The month of March saw considerable outflows in XLP (XLP).

The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) outperformed the broader market, delivering gains of close to 5.5% as investors sought stability in companies with predictable earnings and pricing power.

Within staples, performance was sharply divided, with upstream food suppliers and large retailers leading gains. Bunge Global (BG) surged more than 40% in the quarter, while Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) advanced over 23%, supported by strong commodity trading and processing margins. Retailers including Target (TGT) and Kroger (KR) rose about 22% and 18%, respectively, benefiting from resilient consumer demand and improved traffic trends.

Top 5 performers (Q1 2026)

  • Bunge Global (BG)  +40.81%
  • Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +23.35%
  • Target (TGT) +21.91%
  • Kroger (KR) +17.61%
  • Hershey (HSY) +17.21%

Bottom 5 performers (Q1 2026)

  • Estée Lauder (EL) -35.77%
  • McCormick (MKC) -21.55%
  • Campbell Soup (CPB) -20.96%
  • General Mills (GIS) -20.48%
  • Dollar Tree (DLTR) -14.74%

However, several packaged food companies lagged, reflecting margin pressure and softer volumes. General Mills (GIS), Campbell (CPB), and McCormick each fell more than 20% during the quarter. Discount retailers Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Dollar General (DG) also declined sharply, signaling ongoing strain among lower-income consumers. 

The biggest drag came from Estée Lauder (EL), which tumbled nearly 36%, hit by weak international demand and continued pressures in key Asian markets.

SA Analyst comments

"Consumer staples stocks enter 2026 with deeply reset expectations, compressed valuations, and high short interest, creating an asymmetric risk/reward setup." wrote an SA contributor.

"Although the consumer staples sector shares some similarities and is even more resilient when it comes to macroeconomic impacts, I am more bearish on this industry in the short term, and I believe that the current situation is much more complex than it appears to be." said another SA contributor Kenio Fontes.

Other funds for a diversified approach to consumer staples include: Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC); iShares U.S. Consumer Staples ETF (IYK); First Trust Consumer Staples AlphaDEX Fund (FXG); Invesco Dynamic Food & Beverage ETF (PBJ); First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG); iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (KXI).

data.title: ServiceNow's Armis acquisition offers several benefits, Morgan Stanley says
data.description:
• ServiceNow's (NOW) pending deal to acquire Armis could offer several benefits to the Bill McDermott-led enterprise software company once the deal closes, Morgan Stanley said.
  • “Shift in M&A philosophy has been an understandable concern, though Armis provides benefits beyond incremental growth potential... ...including broadening the TAM. [Operational technology] has been an area of focus for ServiceNow and Armis further solidifies its positioning in an increasingly important market,” analyst Keith Weiss wrote in a note to clients. “More importantly, Armis enhances ServiceNow's CMDB with robust physical asset data, enhancing ServiceNow's end-to-end workflow capabilities.”
  • Weiss, who has an Overweight rating and a $210 price target on ServiceNow, said the Armis deal can help ServiceNow in four ways: it closes the physical asset visibility gap; it boosts the physical asset library; it improves existing solutions and security capabilities; and it increases end-to-end workflow.
  • “While ServiceNow has succeeded in enhancing their digital data store through expanding horizontally into departments beyond IT and becoming a de-facto enterprise system-of-record, the company's physical asset library has lagged with OT being an area of focus, though a smaller contributor to the business over the past several years,” Weiss explained. “Armis expands upon the latter opportunity, providing robust physical asset data that ServiceNow can integrate into their CMDB giving enterprises a unified view of digital and physical environments. This unification subsequently enhances ServiceNow's existing solutions and security capabilities with specific workflow offerings such as ITAM, ITSM, ITOM, SecOps, and IRM/GRC well-positioned to benefit.”

data.title: Lamb Weston forecasts $6.45B-$6.55B in fiscal 2026 net sales while targeting $250M in cost savings by fiscal 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lamb Weston (LW) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “In the third quarter, we delivered another solid performance... The fifth quarter in a row of in line or better results,” supported by “our updated fiscal 2026 outlook including a tighter guidance range and a higher midpoint of net sales and EBITDA,” Mike Smith said (President, CEO & Director Mike Smith).
  • Smith highlighted North America momentum, saying “customer wins, share gains and strong retention delivered 12% volume growth and 5% net sales growth in the segment,” while adding the company grew “while restaurant traffic and consumer sentiment have been soft” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • On international actions, Smith said the company is responding to a European surplus and softer demand, noting it “announced the closure of our Munro, Argentina plant,” began “temporarily curtailing our production line in the Netherlands,” and “doesn't plan to resume production in 1 of its previously curtailed Australia locations” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Smith described the strategy shift: “This strategy is a departure from Lamb Weston's previous focus on growth and scale,” adding it is now “a customer and return-centric framework,” and reiterated the cost program: “we set a target of $250 million in cost savings by fiscal year-end 2028” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • “Third quarter net sales increased 3%, including a $47 million benefit from foreign currency translation,” and “adjusted EBITDA declined $101 million compared to last year, to $272 million,” Bernadette Madarieta said (Chief Financial Officer Bernadette Madarieta).

Outlook

  • The company raised and narrowed fiscal 2026 guidance: “We currently expect net sales in the range of $6.45 billion to $6.55 billion,” and “adjusted EBITDA is now expected to be in the range of $1.08 billion to $1.14 billion,” Madarieta said (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • For Q4 profitability cadence, management said: “Adjusted gross margin is expected to decline seasonally in the fourth quarter, down 250 to 300 basis points from the third quarter's 20.9%,” with the EBITDA outlook including “our current assessment of the additional risk associated with the ongoing Middle East conflict” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management moved from “reaffirming our fiscal 2026 outlook” to tightening the ranges and “raising the low end of our net sales guidance and increasing the midpoint” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).

Financial Results

  • Reported quarter details included: “Volume increased 7%,” while “price/mix declined 7% at constant currency,” reflecting “targeted investments in our customers for price and trade support” and “adverse product mix,” Madarieta said (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • Segment performance showed North America strength but margin pressure: “North America net sales increased 5%... Volume increased 12%,” while “price/mix declined 7%,” and segment “adjusted EBITDA declined 4%... to $290 million,” Madarieta said (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • International softness was tied to EMEA: “At constant currency, net sales declined 9%,” “volume declined 2%,” and “adjusted EBITDA declined $76 million to $19 million,” with drivers including “a $33 million net pretax charge to write off excess raw potatoes” and “underutilized production facilities” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • Cash flow and capital intensity shifted: “Through the first 3 quarters of fiscal 2026, we generated $596 million of cash from operations,” and “we delivered $339 million year-to-date in free cash flow,” while “capital expenditures were $257 million in the first 3 quarters,” and “We now estimate full year cash spend to be approximately $400 million” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).

Q&A

  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Asked about utilization and whether international actions move the company toward “90-plus percent”; Mike Smith replied, “Overall, in North America, we're in the low 90s,” and said international moves include curtailments and the Argentina consolidation, while emphasizing plant capability differences mean “it's not as easy as just turning off 1 line” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Asked about Europe pricing and cheap spot potatoes; Smith said Europe pressure is “a combination of multiple factors” including “capacity imbalance,” “slower demand,” and “the crop,” and added, “With the reset for this year, we are contracting less acres” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Asked about North America price/mix trajectory into fiscal 2027; Smith said, “our expectation is that we're going to continue to have some price mix pressure in fiscal '27,” and “We'll provide guidance on fiscal '27 like we normally do with our Q4 earnings” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities: Asked about reduced CapEx and structural environmental spend; Smith said “we have enough capacity in our footprint,” and Madarieta added, “we have spent the $100 million that we anticipated spending on environmental expenditures this year” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • Matthew Smith, Stifel: Asked if the company becomes more “choiceful about volume”; Smith said normalized utilization “does allow us to be more thoughtful about the business we pick up” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Matthew Smith, Stifel: Asked about incremental potato write-offs; Smith answered, “We don't anticipate additional raw write-offs” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: Asked about competitors’ capacity ramp; Smith said, “I can't speak to their production,” but added “we're winning” with customers (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Alexia Howard, Bernstein: Asked whether Europe potato write-offs can be avoided; Smith said, “we have made some adjustments in how we're procuring raw in Europe... to be a little bit smarter and give us a little bit more flexibility” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Asked how much North America curtailed capacity remains; Smith said, “we've restarted most of our curtailed lines” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Marc Torrente, Wells Fargo Securities: Asked where incremental cost savings are coming from; Smith said savings are “actually hitting the cost side, supply chain side of things” and that the company will share future plans “at the right time” (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Carla Casella, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: Asked about Middle East conflict cost disruption; Smith cited risks from “lower volumes,” inventories, and “increased volatility” in “packaging, fuel,” and Madarieta noted the company “hedge portions of our key inputs to reduce volatility” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical, with repeated probing on utilization targets, Europe pricing mechanics, durability of North America price/mix, the risk of repeat write-offs, and conflict-related cost exposure.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing controllables and deferring fiscal 2027 specifics to Q4; Smith used firm language on certain items, including, “We don't anticipate additional raw write-offs,” while also qualifying uncertainty around conflict duration and severity (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Versus the prior quarter, guidance language was more confident on near-term delivery (tighter ranges and higher midpoints), while Q&A continued to carry similar pressure on international capacity/pricing and North America mix; leadership-change messaging was materially more prominent this quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from Q2’s “reaffirming our fiscal 2026 outlook” and expecting to finish “closer to the midpoint” of a wider adjusted EBITDA range to Q3’s tighter outlook of $1.08B-$1.14B adjusted EBITDA and $6.45B-$6.55B net sales (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • International posture became more action-oriented: Q2 discussed curtailing “a single line” in Europe and ramp costs in Argentina, while Q3 detailed the Munro, Argentina closure and consolidation, Netherlands curtailment beginning in Q4, and not resuming one curtailed Australia location (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Leadership updates intensified: Smith introduced “Our new Executive Chair, Jan Craps,” and said, “We will also soon welcome Jim Gray, our incoming CFO,” while Madarieta said, “This is my final call as CFO” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • Analyst focus remained consistent, centered on North America price/mix durability and international supply/demand balance, with added emphasis this quarter on write-offs and Middle East conflict risk embedded in guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited international pressure from “a significant surplus in the European potato market,” “persistently lower restaurant traffic,” and “local sourcing” reducing exports, with mitigation via plant closures, curtailments, and capital discipline (President, CEO & Director Smith).
  • Madarieta identified profitability headwinds including “unfavorable price/mix,” the “$33 million net pretax charge” in International, “underutilized production facilities,” and input inflation, while pointing to cost savings and tariff changes as offsets, including “we recognized approximately $4 million of tariff expenses” and “we don't expect to incur this cost after the fourth quarter” if agreements hold (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).
  • The company flagged Middle East conflict exposure in volumes and cost volatility; Madarieta said the updated EBITDA range “includes our current assessment of the additional risk” (Chief Financial Officer Madarieta).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q3 as another in a series of “in line or better” quarters and used the North America volume-driven performance to support tighter and raised fiscal 2026 guidance ranges, while acknowledging international profitability headwinds from European oversupply, weaker traffic, and the Middle East conflict risk. Leadership change (incoming CFO Jim Gray and Executive Chair Jan Craps) and decisive footprint actions in Argentina, the Netherlands, and Australia were positioned as key enablers of the “Focus to Win” shift toward returns, cost savings, and more disciplined capital deployment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: United Natural Foods' local union members authorize strike
data.description:
Warehouse employees at United Natural Foods’ (UNFI) Pompano Beach, Florida facility have voted unanimously to go on strike if the company does not offer a fair contract and stop its unfair labor practices.

United Natural Foods (UNFI) is the primary distributor for Amazon’s (AMZN) Whole Foods supermarkets.

The Pompano-based employees voted last year to join the powerful Teamsters union, which seeks higher wages, stronger benefits, and safer working conditions for its 5,500 members from United Natural Foods (UNFI).

"Management needs to get serious, stop breaking the law, and put a real contract on the table—or they'll be dealing with the ramifications of a strike," Local 769 President Josh Zivalich said.

“UNFI can either deliver a fair first contract, or they can face the consequences. If this company keeps stalling, Teamsters will be in the streets," added Tom Erickson, director of the Teamsters Warehouse Division. 

Seeking Alpha reached out to representatives from United Natural Foods (UNFI) for comment.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Lifeway Foods soars after raising guidance on strong demand trends
data.description:
Lifeway Foods (LWAY) soared in Wednesday afternoon trading after the Illinois-based company issued preliminary first-quarter results.

The health food company expects sales for Q1 to be in the range of $60.8M to $62.3M, which would mark a year-over-year increase of 32% to 35% and easily top the consensus estimate of analysts. 

The strong sales quarter was noted to reflect continued momentum across Lifeway's (LWAY) core kefir and farmer cheese portfolio, with strong consumer demand for protein-rich, probiotic foods.

"We are entering 2026 with strong velocity across our business and continued demand for Lifeway's category-leading kefir and farmer cheese," highlighted CEO Julie Smolyansky. "Our focus remains on expanding distribution, driving innovation, and delivering consistent, profitable growth as we build on our leadership in the fermented dairy space," she added.

Notably, Lifeway Foods (LWAY) has recorded six consecutive years of volume-led annual sales growth.

Shares of Lifeway Foods (LWAY) were up 15.3% to $22.30 at 2:04 p.m. vs. the 52-week range of $17.31 to $34.20.
